1:
ἓξ ἡμέραι πρὸ τοῦ πάσχα — "six days before the Passover." The Evangelist's precise temporal marker ties Jesus' arrival at Bethany to the Passover calendar. Under Levitical practice the Passover lamb was selected on Nisan 10 — exactly six days before Nisan 14, the day of slaughter. The timing is unlikely to be accidental.

ὃν ἤγειρεν ἐκ νεκρῶν — "whom he raised from the dead," aorist of egeirō (G1453). The clause is explanatory — the Evangelist re-anchors Lazarus as the embodied evidence that draws the crowd (John 12:9), making Bethany the staging point for the final confrontation.
2:
ἐδιακόνει — imperfect of diakoneō (G1247): "was serving." The imperfect marks the service as ongoing throughout the meal, not a single act. Martha's characteristic activity (Luke 10:40 uses the same verb) appears here without contrast or rebuke — the dinner context is hospitable, not polemical.

ἀνακειμένων — present participle of anakeimai (G345), "reclining at table." Formal Passover preparation and Greco-Roman banquet practice both called for reclining, distinguishing festive meals from ordinary eating. Lazarus reclining alongside Jesus underlines the physical reality of the resurrection.
3:
λίτραν μύρου νάρδου πιστικῆς — litra (G3046) is the Roman pound (~327g, a large quantity). Nardos (spikenard, from the Himalayan plant Nardostachys jatamansi) was imported from India and priced accordingly. Pistikēs (G4101, related to pistis) most probably means "genuine" or "unadulterated" — a quality marker, not a place-name. The construction means "a pound of genuine, costly spikenard."

ἤλειψεν τοὺς πόδας — "anointed the feet," aleiphō (G218), the more common term for anointing with oil or perfume (as opposed to the sacred-appointment sense of chriō). Anointing feet rather than the head is unusual and underlines the extravagance and humility simultaneously.

ἐπλήρωθη ἡ οἰκία — "the house was filled" (plēroō, G4137, aorist passive). The filling of the house with fragrance is the Evangelist's detail that no other account shares — functioning as a sensory witness to the scene's excess.
4: ὁ μέλλων αὐτὸν παραδιδόναι — "the one about to betray him," present participle of mellō + infinitive of paradidōmi (G3860). The participle is progressive: the betrayal plan is already underway, not a future contingency. The Evangelist introduces Judas here, before his objection, to prime the reader to hear the "concern for the poor" as performance rather than conviction.
5: τριακοσίων δηναρίων — "three hundred denarii." A denarius was the standard daily wage for a laborer (Matthew 20:2), making 300 denarii roughly a full year's earnings. Judas's calculation correctly identifies the extravagance, which is the point — the anointing is deliberately disproportionate.
6:
κλέπτης ἦν — "he was a thief," kleptēs (G2812), imperfect of eimi. The imperfect marks this as an established characteristic, not a new development. The Evangelist's retrospective comment here is the only place in the Gospels where Judas is called a thief outright.

τὸ γλωσσόκομον εἶχεν — "he had the money bag." Glōssokomon (G1101) originally referred to a case for mouthpieces of reed instruments, then repurposed for any small box or bag. The imperfect eichen again marks it as a continuing arrangement — Judas managed the group's finances habitually.

ἐβάσταζεν — "was stealing/carrying," bastazō (G941). The verb means both "to carry" (his legitimate role) and in Hellenistic usage "to pilfer" — the Evangelist deploys the ambiguity deliberately. Both senses are likely intended: he carried the bag and carried off its contents.
7:
ἄφες αὐτήν — "leave her alone," aorist imperative of aphiēmi (G863), abrupt and final. The command overrides Judas entirely and defends Mary without explanation of her motives.

εἰς τὴν ἡμέραν τοῦ ἐνταφιασμοῦ τετήρηκεν αὐτό — "she has kept it for the day of my burial." Entaphiasmos (G1780, preparation for burial) occurs in NT only here and Matthew 26:12. Tetērēken is perfect active of tēreō (G5083) — "she has kept/reserved it." Whether Mary consciously acted for the burial or Jesus reinterprets her act proleptically is intentionally ambiguous; the syntax allows both readings. What the Evangelist underlines is that the anointing is oriented toward death, not celebration.
8:
τοὺς πτωχοὺς πάντοτε ἔχετε μεθ' ἑαυτῶν — "the poor you always have with you," echoing Deuteronomy 15:11 ("for there will never cease to be poor in the land"). The allusion does not demean the poor or charitable care for them; it situates the present moment as exceptional — this particular act, at this particular moment, is unrepeatable.

ἐμὲ δὲ οὐ πάντοτε ἔχετε — "but me you do not always have." The contrast is temporal: the anointing is possible only now, before the death Jesus is moving toward.
9: ἔγνω ὄχλος πολύς — "a large crowd learned" (aorist of ginōskō, G1097). The crowd's motivation is split: not only Jesus but Lazarus — the Evangelist signals that the resurrection of Lazarus has become an independent attraction, complicating the authorities' attempts to manage public response.
10: ἵνα καὶ τὸν Λάζαρον ἀποκτείνωσιν — "in order that they might kill Lazarus also," purpose clause with aorist subjunctive of apokteinō (G615). The logic of silencing a living sign by killing it is grimly ironic — killing the one Jesus raised would only re-demonstrate the claim. The chief priests' decision is self-defeating, but fear overrides reason.
11:
ὅτι πολλοὶ δι᾽ αὐτὸν ὑπῆγον — "because many on account of him were going away," imperfect of hypagō (G5217). The imperfect marks a gradual, ongoing defection from the Jerusalem leadership's sphere of influence. Hypagō can mean simply "to go" but in Johannine usage often implies movement from one sphere to another (cf. John 8:21).

ἐπίστευον εἰς τὸν Ἰησοῦν — "were believing in Jesus," imperfect again — the belief is spreading incrementally, not as a single event.
12:
τῇ ἐπαύριον — "the next day," marking the scene shift from the intimate dinner at Bethany to the public approach to Jerusalem. The movement from private to public is a Johannine structural feature across the passion week.

ὁ ὄχλος πολύς ὁ ἐλθὼν εἰς τὴν ἑορτήν — "the large crowd that had come to the festival." The antecedent crowd is the Passover pilgrimage crowd, distinct from the Bethany observers. Both groups converge for the triumphal entry.
13:
βαΐα τῶν φοινίκων — "branches of palm trees," baia (G902) appears only here in NT. Palms were carried at the Feast of Tabernacles (Leviticus 23:40) and were a symbol of Jewish national and military victory (1 Macc John 13:51; 2 Macc John 10:7). Waving palms at Passover time, when Tabernacles palms were not customary, is a politically charged gesture.

ὡσαννά — hōsanna (G5614), a transliteration of Hebrew הוֹשִׁיעָה נָּא, "save, pray!" (Psalms 118:25). By the first century it had become a liturgical acclamation rather than a petition, roughly "hail!" or "praise God!" — though its original intercessory meaning likely lingered.

εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου — Psalms 118:26 LXX, the Hallel psalm sung at Passover. The crowd adds ὁ βασιλεὺς τοῦ Ἰσραήλ (king of Israel), an interpretive expansion not in the psalm text, making explicit the messianic reading.
14:
εὑρὼν δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὀνάριον — "having found a young donkey," aorist participle; onarion (G3678) is a diminutive — "little donkey" — found only here in NT. The act of finding and riding is understated; the Evangelist is pointing the reader to the prophetic text rather than the logistics.

ἐκάθισεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτό — "sat on it," aorist of kathizō (G2523). The action fulfills the prophetic pattern; the brief narrative keeps attention on the significance, not the mechanics.
15:
Μὴ φοβοῦ, θύγατερ Σιών — "Do not fear, daughter Zion." The MT of Zechariah 9:9 reads "Rejoice greatly" (גִּילִי), but the LXX reads "rejoice" differently, and John's version "do not fear" may follow a variant LXX tradition or paraphrase. The address "daughter Zion" (a personification of Jerusalem) is retained.

ἐπὶ πῶλον ὄνου — "on a donkey's colt," pōlos (G4454, colt), the same term as the LXX of Zechariah 9:9. John uses onarion in John 12:14 but quotes the LXX's pōlos in the fulfillment citation — a common citation practice: narrative freedom, lexical precision in the quotation.
16:
οὐκ ἔγνωσαν αὐτοῦ οἱ μαθηταί — "his disciples did not understand these things at first," aorist of ginōskō. The disciples' incomprehension at significant moments is a recurring Johannine motif (cf. John 2:22; John 20:9).

ὅτε ἐδοξάσθη Ἰησοῦς — "when Jesus was glorified," aorist passive of doxazō (G1392). In John "glorification" encompasses crucifixion, resurrection, and ascension as a single movement. The post-resurrection retrospect enables the disciples to connect the entry with Zechariah's text.

γεγραμμένα — "written things," perfect passive participle of graphō: "the things standing written." The perfect tense is the Evangelist's standard way of marking scripture as permanently authoritative.
17: ἐμαρτύρει οὖν ὁ ὄχλος — "the crowd was therefore testifying," imperfect of martyreō (G3140). The witnesses to the Lazarus event spread their testimony, generating the crowd of John 12:18. Martyreō is a forensic term — the crowd is functioning as legal witnesses to what they saw.
18: διὰ τοῦτο καὶ ὑπήντησεν αὐτῷ ὁ ὄχλος — "for this reason the crowd met him," aorist of hypantaō (G5221). The Evangelist distinguishes the two crowds: those who went out because of what they heard Lazarus had been raised (John 12:18), and those who witnessed the event (John 12:17). Both groups testify, but their testimony has different epistemic weights.
19: ὁ κόσμος ὀπίσω αὐτοῦ ἀπῆλθεν — "the whole world has gone after him," aorist of aperchomai (G565). The Pharisees' frustrated hyperbole ("the whole world") is immediately followed by the arrival of Greeks (John 12:20), turning their exaggeration into unwitting prophecy. Kosmos in John's usage (cf. John 3:16) carries precisely the universal scope the Pharisees dismiss as an idiom.
20: Ἕλληνές τινες — "certain Greeks," most likely Gentile God-fearers or proselytes who came up to worship at the festival rather than diaspora Greek-speaking Jews. The arrival of Gentiles at this precise moment functions as the external trigger for Jesus' declaration in John 12:23 that "the hour has come."
21:
Φιλίππῳ τῷ ἀπὸ Βηθσαϊδά — Philip from Bethsaida; both Philip and Andrew (John 12:22) bear Greek names, and Bethsaida was a border region with significant Gentile contact, making them natural intermediaries. The Evangelist's specificity here is not incidental — these disciples become the channel through which the Gentile approach reaches Jesus.

ἠρώτων αὐτόν — "they were asking him," imperfect — repeated or persistent asking. ἰδεῖν τὸν Ἰησοῦν — "to see Jesus." Theloō + horaō infinitive; in Johannine idiom "to see" often carries the weight of genuine encounter with Jesus' identity (cf. John 1:39, John 6:36).
22: ἔρχεται Φίλιππος καὶ λέγει τῷ Ἀνδρέᾳ — historical presents (erchomai, legō) vivify the relay. Philip does not go directly to Jesus but consults Andrew first — possibly reflecting uncertainty about how Jesus would receive Gentiles, or a chain-of-command dynamic within the Twelve.
23:
ἐλήλυθεν ἡ ὥρα — "the hour has come," perfect of erchomai (G2064): "it has come and is now here." Throughout John 2–11, "the hour" of Jesus was either not yet come (John 2:4; John 7:30; John 8:20) or approaching (John 4:21, 23; John 5:25, 28). The Gentiles' request marks the transition: the universal mission Jesus is about to complete through death is now imminent.

ἵνα δοξασθῇ ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — purpose clause: "that the Son of Man might be glorified," aorist passive subjunctive of doxazō. The response to the Greeks' desire to "see" Jesus is not an audience but an announcement of cross-death as the very means of glorification.
24:
ὁ κόκκος τοῦ σίτου — "the kernel of wheat," kokkos sitou (G2848, G4621). The grain-of-wheat image is Jesus' own interpretation of his death — not merely loss but necessary precondition for fruitfulness. The logic is agricultural: buried seed is not wasted but productive.

ἐὰν μὴ πέσῃ εἰς τὴν γῆν καὶ ἀποθάνῃ — "unless it falls into the earth and dies," two aorist subjunctives in a conditional. Apothnēskō (G599) is used for both literal death and the grain's ending — the metaphor turns on the word.

μόνος μένει — "it remains alone," monos (G3441) + present of menō (G3306, to abide/remain). The contrast with polun karpon pherei (bears much fruit) is absolute: isolation vs. abundance, death vs. multiplication.
25:
ὁ φιλῶν τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ ἀπολλύει αὐτήν — "the one loving his life loses it." Psychē (G5590) appears in both clauses, creating the paradox that hinges on one word. Phileō (G5368, love with attachment) and miseō (G3404, hate) form the antithesis; but miseo here means "value less by comparison," a Semitic idiom of relative priority (cf. Luke 14:26).

εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον φυλάξει αὐτήν — "will keep it for eternal life," future of phylassō (G5442). The keeping in view of zōē aiōnios (G2222 + G166) is the governing purpose — the apparent self-destruction is actually preservation at the highest level.
26:
ἐμοί τις διακονῇ, ἐμοὶ ἀκολουθείτω — "if anyone serves me, let him follow me," present subjunctive + present imperative. Diakoneō (G1247) and akoloutheō (G190) are paired: service to Jesus is inseparable from the pattern of following him — including through death.

ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγώ, ἐκεῖ καὶ ὁ διάκονος ὁ ἐμὸς ἔσται — "where I am, there also will my servant be." The ἐγώ is emphatic; the promise of co-location with Jesus is the reward of servant-following.

τιμήσει αὐτόν — "will honor him," future of timaō (G5091). The Father's honoring of Christ's servant is the direct counterpart of the shame/dishonor that public discipleship brought in an honor-shame culture.
27:
ἡ ψυχή μου τετάρακται — "my soul has been troubled," perfect of tarassō (G5015). The perfect marks a present state resulting from a past disturbance — Jesus is not merely observing a coming trouble but is already in it. The same verb appears at John 11:33 (the tomb of Lazarus) and John 13:21 (announcing the betrayal) — tarachthē marks the moments of deepest human cost in the narrative.

τί εἴπω; πάτερ, σῶσόν με ἐκ τῆς ὥρας ταύτης — the prayer-question is interrogative, not an actual petition: "What should I say? 'Father, save me from this hour'?" The rhetorical structure immediately overturns the possibility: ἀλλὰ διὰ τοῦτο ἦλθον εἰς τὴν ὥρα ταύτην — "but for this very reason I came to this hour." This is the Johannine equivalent of the Synoptic Gethsemane — the struggle is present but the submission is immediate.
28:
δόξασόν σου τὸ ὄνομα — "glorify your name," aorist imperative of doxazō. The request is not for deliverance but for the Father's glorification — a reorientation from John 12:27's implicit plea.

ἐδόξασα καὶ πάλιν δοξάσω — "I have glorified and will glorify again." The double aorist-future response from heaven (cf. John 12:30) encompasses past (the entire ministry) and future (the passion and exaltation). Edoxasa is aorist — a completed glorification; doxasō is future — the completing act is imminent.
29: βροντὴν γεγονέναι… ἄγγελος αὐτῷ λελάληκεν — "that it had thundered… that an angel had spoken to him." The perfect infinitives (gegonēnai from ginomai, lelalēken from laleō) report completed events. The crowd's divided response — thunder vs. angel — illustrates the Johannine motif of differential hearing: the voice was audible but only partially intelligible.
30: δι᾽ ὑμᾶς αὕτη ἡ φωνὴ γέγονεν, οὐ δι᾽ ἐμέ — "this voice has occurred for your sake, not for mine," perfect of ginomai. Jesus does not need the audible confirmation — it is for the witnesses. The statement implies that Jesus' own confidence in the Father's glorification does not depend on public divine speech.
31:
νῦν κρίσις ἐστὶν τοῦ κόσμου τούτου — "now is the judgment of this world," krisis (G2920) in its judicial sense. The cross is the moment of cosmic adjudication — not the final judgment of all things but the definitive crisis in which the world's allegiance is exposed and the ruler's power broken.

ὁ ἄρχων τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἐκβληθήσεται ἔξω — "the ruler of this world will be cast out," future passive of ekballō (G1544). Archōn (G758, ruler) is used for the devil/Satan in John (John 14:30; John 16:11) — a supernatural power over the world system, whose authority is broken at the cross, not merely threatened.
32:
κἀγὼ ἐὰν ὑψωθῶ ἐκ τῆς γῆς — "and I, if I am lifted up from the earth," aorist passive subjunctive of hypsoō (G5312). Hypsoō in John carries double freight: literal elevation on a cross (John 3:14; John 8:28) and figurative exaltation to glory. The cross is simultaneously degradation and enthronement in Johannine theology.

πάντας ἑλκύσω πρὸς ἐμαυτόν — "I will draw all people to myself," future of helkyō (G1670, to drag/draw). Pantas (all people, accusative plural) reaches beyond Israel — triggered precisely by the Greeks' arrival (John 12:20–21). The drawing is not automatic universalism but a statement of intended scope; cf. 6:44 where the Father draws those who will come.
33: σημαίνων ποίῳ θανάτῳ ἤμελλεν ἀποθνῄσκειν — "signifying by what kind of death he was about to die," sēmainō (G4591, to indicate by sign or signal). The Evangelist's editorial comment connects "lifted up" (John 12:32) to crucifixion explicitly — completing the pattern established at 3:14 and John 8:28.
34:
ἡμεῖς ἠκούσαμεν ἐκ τοῦ νόμου ὅτι ὁ Χριστὸς μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "we have heard from the Law that the Christ remains forever." The crowd's appeal to "the Law" invokes texts like Psalms 89:36–37 and Isaiah 9:7 (the eternal Davidic throne). They cannot reconcile "lifted up" with eternal messianic reign.

τίς ἐστιν οὗτος ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — "who is this Son of Man?" The question reveals that the crowd understands "Son of Man" as a messianic title but cannot integrate the death-trajectory with it. Their nomos hermeneutic is correct in its data but wrong in its interpretive frame.
35:
ἔτι μικρὸν χρόνον τὸ φῶς ἐν ὑμῖν ἐστιν — "still a little time the light is among you," mikros (G3398) + chronos (G5550). Jesus does not answer the crowd's question directly but shifts to urgent appeal. The temporal pressure ("a little while") is a characteristic Johannine device (John 7:33; John 13:33; John 16:16).

περιπατεῖτε ὡς τὸ φῶς ἔχετε, ἵνα μὴ σκοτία ὑμᾶς καταλάβῃ — "walk while you have the light, so that darkness may not overtake you." Katalambanō (G2638, to seize/overtake) gives darkness an active, pursuing quality. The imperative peripateite (G4043, present — ongoing walking) frames discipleship as lived movement.
36:
πιστεύετε εἰς τὸ φῶς, ἵνα υἱοὶ φωτὸς γένησθε — "believe in the light, so that you may become sons of light," aorist subjunctive of ginomai. Hyioi phōtos (sons of light) is a Semitic idiom denoting people characterized by and belonging to the light — not just illuminated but constituted by it (cf. 1 Thessalonians 5:5; Ephesians 5:8).

ἀπελθὼν ἐκρύβη ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν — "having gone away, he hid himself from them," aorist of kryptō (G2928). The withdrawal marks the close of Jesus' public teaching; chapters 13–17 are private. The hiddenness also fulfills John 12:35's warning about the light's departure.
37: τοσαῦτα δὲ αὐτοῦ σημεῖα πεποιηκότος ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν οὐκ ἐπίστευον εἰς αὐτόν — "although he had done so many signs in their presence, they were not believing in him," imperfect of pisteuō. The perfect participle pepoiēkotos underlines the completed, abundant evidence; the imperfect episteuon marks the unbelief as an ongoing state in the face of it. Volume of evidence does not produce faith.
38: ἵνα ὁ λόγος Ἠσαΐου τοῦ προφήτου πληρωθῇ — "that the word/oracle of Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled." Logos (G3056, disp=3) here means "oracle" or "prophetic word" — not the metaphysical Logos of 1:1 but the spoken word of Isaiah. Isaiah 53:1 LXX is quoted essentially verbatim: κύριε, τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν — "Lord, who believed our report?" Akoē (G189) means both "hearing" and "report/message heard" — the LXX renders the Hebrew שְׁמֻעָתֵנוּ accurately.
39: διὰ τοῦτο οὐκ ἠδύναντο πιστεύειν — "for this reason they were not able to believe." Dynamai (G1410) + infinitive of pisteuō — a statement of incapacity, not merely unwillingness. The Evangelist is not eliminating human responsibility but is tracing the theological logic of Isaiah's text: the hardening is divinely permitted and scripturally anticipated.
40:
τετύφλωκεν αὐτῶν τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς — "he has blinded their eyes," perfect of typhloō (G5186). The divine subject of both verbs (blinds, hardens) in the Isaiah 6:10 citation is theologically difficult. Isa 6 depicts Isaiah's commissioning with a message that will produce judicial hardening — not that God wills unbelief as such but that the word of God exposes and confirms the orientation of the heart.

ἐπώρωσεν αὐτῶν τὴν καρδίαν — "has hardened their hearts," perfect of pōroō (G4456), from pōros (hard stone, callus). The perfect tense in both verbs signals a state now established, not an ongoing process. John's version of the Isaiah 6:10 quotation differs from the LXX and from Matthew 13:14-15 — it may follow a different textual tradition or be a paraphrase.
41:
ταῦτα εἶπεν Ἠσαΐας ὅτι εἶδεν τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ — "Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory," aorist of horaō (G3708). The pronoun autou (his) refers to Jesus — the Evangelist's astonishing exegetical claim is that the vision of Yahweh enthroned in Isaiah 6:1-3 was a vision of Christ's pre-incarnate glory. This is one of the highest Christological statements in the Gospel, made almost in passing.

καὶ ἐλάλησεν περὶ αὐτοῦ — "and he spoke about him," aorist of laleō. The entire Isaiah 6 commission — including the hardening oracle — is understood as speech about Christ.
42:
ὅμως μέντοι καὶ ἐκ τῶν ἀρχόντων πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν — "nevertheless even many of the rulers believed in him." Homōs mentoi — a rare particle combination in NT (only here), meaning "nevertheless/nonetheless even so." The believing rulers are a significant qualification to the universal unbelief of John 12:37–40.

διὰ τοὺς Φαρισαίους οὐχ ὡμολόγουν — "because of the Pharisees they were not confessing," imperfect of homologeō (G3670, to confess/declare openly). The fear is institutional: the Pharisees have authority to exclude (cf. John 9:22).
43: ἠγάπησαν γὰρ τὴν δόξαν τῶν ἀνθρώπων μᾶλλον ἤπερ τὴν δόξαν τοῦ θεοῦ — "for they loved the glory of men more than the glory of God," aorist of agapaō (G25). The comparison mallon ēper (more than) reveals not absence of belief but divided loyalty. Doxa (G1391) appears twice — the same word, differently sourced: human approval vs. divine honor. In a shame/honor culture this is the ultimate diagnosis: miscalibrated honor attachment.
44:
Ἰησοῦς δὲ ἔκραξεν καὶ εἶπεν — "Jesus cried out and said," aorist of krazō (G2896, to cry out loud, often of urgent proclamation). The verb marks a public declaration rather than private teaching — a formal announcement after his withdrawal (John 12:36).

ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ οὐ πιστεύει εἰς ἐμὲ ἀλλὰ εἰς τὸν πέμψαντά με — "the one believing in me does not believe in me only, but in the one who sent me." The construction is not paradoxical — it is relational: faith in Jesus is not isolated to his person but reaches through him to the Father. Pempsanta (aorist participle of pempō, G3992, to send) specifies the Father's active commissioning.
45: ὁ θεωρῶν ἐμὲ θεωρεῖ τὸν πέμψαντά με — "the one seeing me sees the one who sent me," present participles. Theōreō (G2334) is the more intensive verb for seeing — to observe, behold, contemplate. The statement extends the faith-transparency of John 12:44 to vision: to look at Jesus is to look at the Father. Compare John 14:9.
46: ἐγὼ φῶς εἰς τὸν κόσμον ἐλήλυθα — "I have come as light into the world," perfect of erchomai — "I have come and remain present as light." The light/world declaration echoes the prologue (John 1:4-9) and the self-declarations at 8:12 and John 9:5, now given as the culminating public statement of Jesus' ministry.
47:
καὶ ἐάν τίς μου ἀκούσῃ τῶν ῥημάτων καὶ μὴ φυλάξῃ — "if anyone hears my words and does not keep them," aorist subjunctives (akousē, phylaxē). Both hearing and keeping are conditional; neither alone defines the relationship to Jesus' words.

οὐ κρίνω αὐτόν — "I do not judge him." The first-person negation is emphatic. The mission of the first coming is soteriological, not judicial — the contrast between 3:17 and the final judgment is repeated here.
48:
ὁ ἀθετῶν ἐμέ — "the one rejecting me," present participle of atheteō (G114, to set aside, refuse, reject — often of rejecting what has authority). The one who rejects is contrasted with the one who merely fails to keep (John 12:47); atheteō is active refusal rather than passive neglect.

ὁ λόγος ὃν ἐλάλησα, ἐκεῖνος κρινεῖ αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ — "the word I have spoken, that word will judge him on the last day." The word (logos) is the instrument of judgment — not an external law imposed but the standard Jesus himself established in his ministry. The word stands as witness against those who rejected it.
49:
ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐξ ἐμαυτοῦ οὐκ ἐλάλησα — "because I have not spoken from myself," ek emautou (from myself/on my own authority). The divine origin of Jesus' words is the ground for their judicial authority — they are not his private opinion but the Father's commission.

ὁ πέμψας με πατήρ αὐτός μοι ἐντολὴν δέδωκεν — "the Father who sent me himself has given me commandment," perfect of didōmi — "has given and the commandment stands." Entolē (G1785, commandment) frames the Father's direction of Jesus' speech as a standing directive.
50:
ἡ ἐντολὴ αὐτοῦ ζωὴ αἰώνιός ἐστιν — "his commandment is eternal life," zōē aiōnios (G2222 + G166). The equation is remarkable: the Father's instruction is not a path to eternal life but constitutes it. Obedience to the Father and eternal life are identified, not merely connected.

καθὼς εἴρηκέν μοι ὁ πατήρ, οὕτως λαλῶ — "as the Father has spoken to me, so I speak," perfect of legō. The perfect eirēken marks the Father's word as the permanent source from which Jesus' present speaking flows.
